pi_items.source: Verified via web search - these Reynolds numbers are standard for aerodynamic analysis of full-scale aircraft and wind turbine applications. NACA literature documents analysis at these exact Re numbers.

QBlade documentation (https://docs.qblade.org/src/user/airfoil/polar_extrapolation.html) explicitly shows 360-degree extrapolation output ranges from -180 to +180 degrees. The example polar file in the documentation demonstrates AOA data spanning from -180.000000 to 180.000000 degrees.
QBlade documentation confirms standard 360-degree polar extrapolation uses -180 to +180 degree range, not -170 to 170.

NACA 4415 is a real, documented 4-digit NACA airfoil with 4% camber at 40% chord and 15% maximum thickness. Verified through multiple sources including NASA NTRS, aerodynamic research papers, and airfoil tools databases.
Ncrit=9 is the standard 'e^9 method' corresponding to average wind tunnel conditions with typical freestream turbulence level of 0.07%. Verified through XFoil documentation and wind tunnel standards.
Viterna method is well-documented for 360-degree polar extrapolation in QBlade and originates from Viterna & Janetzke's 1982 NASA work. First option for extrapolation in QBlade.
Task description specifies XFoil analysis range of -5 to 20 degrees before extrapolation, which is standard for aerodynamic analysis before 360-degree extrapolation.
File naming convention is standard for QBlade/XFoil polar exports. .dat format is confirmed as standard polar file format for QBlade. The suffix '_360' indicates 360-degree extrapolation.
QBlade project files use .wpa extension (QBlade Project Archive). Confirmed through task_utils.sh which lists project files as '*.wpa'.

privileged_info_summary: NACA 4415 is a verified real airfoil (4% camber at 40% chord, 15% thickness). Reynolds numbers 1M, 3M, 5M are standard for full-scale aerodynamic analysis. XFoil Ncrit=9 is standard for wind tunnel conditions. Viterna method is the documented approach for 360-degree polar extrapolation in QBlade. The task generates synthetic data through QBlade simulations. CRITICAL DISCREPANCY FOUND: Metadata claims alpha range of -170 to 170 degrees (340° total), but QBlade documentation explicitly specifies that 360-degree extrapolation produces -180 to 180 degree range (360° total).
